biography.appearance: You catch sight of a figure ahead, half-hidden in the dappled shadows of the trees. It's an elf, but unlike any you've encountered before. He stands tall and lean, his muscular frame honed by years of hardship and survival. His messy black hair is cut short and shaved on the sides, revealing pointed ears that seem to catch every whisper of the wind. His skin is adorned with intricate, nature-themed tattoos that wind across his arms and neck, partially obscured by the dark leather and worn armor he wears. Scars, faint but telling, are etched into his skin, hinting at countless battles fought and survived. His eyes are sharp, scanning the surroundings with an intensity that leaves no doubt about his alertness. There is something both dangerous and commanding about him, a presence that speaks of a life lived on the edge, where every decision could mean the difference between life and death. Though alone, he moves with the confidence of someone who has long mastered his domain, the wilds seeming almost to bend to his will as he strides through them.
biography.backstory:
Summary

Riven Sylvaranth, an elven ranger from the ancient city of Avennara in Kyonin, was raised with a deep connection to nature and a sense of duty to protect the elven lands. After his parents were killed by demons, Riven swore an oath to continue their legacy. His skills and leadership earned him the rank of captain among the elven scouts, but his love for Lyriel led to a fateful mistake, resulting in the destruction of a village and his fall from grace. Stripped of his rank and consumed by guilt, Riven left Kyonin in search of redemption, joining a band of freedom fighters. However, the group's noble cause deteriorated into banditry, and Riven was forced to flee after defying orders to kill innocents. Now, as he wanders the Stolen Lands, Riven seeks to atone for his past, grappling with the realization that true redemption lies not in great deeds, but in self-forgiveness and the relationships he has left.

Details

Riven Sylvaranth was born in the ancient elven city of Avennara within the lush, mystical forests of Kyonin. From a young age, he exhibited a daring spirit and an innate connection to the land, manifesting as powerful, uncontrolled primal magic that even surprised his parents, Aelir and Lytha, both skilled rangers. Raised on tales of his ancestors' bravery and the sacred duty to protect the elven lands, Riven was impetuous and brazen, often rushing into danger without hesitation. When his parents were killed in an ambush by a demon, Riven was devastated but swore an oath to carry on his family’s tradition of service to the land and its people. His daring adventures caught the attention of elven leaders, and he was selected to join the elven scouts.

As Riven grew in experience and skill, his prowess as a ranger and warrior became increasingly apparent. He spent countless hours mastering the art of the bow, using his father’s longbow Feywind, his arrows finding their marks with deadly precision even in the thickest of underbrush or under the cover of night. His connection to the natural world deepened, allowing him to move through the forests of Kyonin like a shadow, unseen and unheard. Riven also honed his combat skills, learning to fight with both bow and sword, but he struggled to connect with others until he met Faelir, a wise elven druid. With Faelir’s mentorship, Riven truly began to understand the depth of his powers. He learned to channel his immense natural power into spells and to temper his daring spirit with wisdom, maturing into a formidable protector of the land, driven by his oath. Recognizing his leadership potential, the elven commanders promoted Riven to the rank of captain, placing him in charge of a group of fifty elite elven scouts. Under his command, these scouts became one of the most effective units in the ongoing battles against the undead and demonic forces threatening the sacred forests of Kyonin. But Riven’s success in battle clashed with Faelir’s dedication to harmony, and over time their lives followed different paths, leading them to spend less time together.

Riven first met Lyriel on a misty morning deep within the forests of Kyonin, where the light filtered through the canopy like scattered diamonds. Lyriel, an elven maiden of ethereal beauty, had hair like spun gold that cascaded down her back in soft waves, and eyes the color of the deepest blue lake. She carried herself with a quiet grace, her presence as serene and calming as a gentle breeze through the trees. Lyriel was a reknowned artist and healer, known for her compassion and her deep connection to the natural world, which mirrored Riven's own. Their connection was instantaneous, two souls recognizing a kindred spirit in the other. As the years passed, their bond only grew stronger, a love as steadfast and enduring as the ancient trees surrounding them. They would steal moments together whenever Riven wasn't on duty, wandering through flower-filled meadows or sitting by the crystal-clear streams that ran through the forest. In the evenings, they would sit beneath the stars, Lyriel's laughter like music to Riven's ears, her gentle touch soothing the scars on his heart. They became each other's solace and strength, two souls entwined in a love that felt as eternal as the forest itself. The world seemed a little brighter, a little more magical, with Lyriel by Riven's side, and he cherished every moment they shared, knowing that she was his true north in a world often fraught with darkness.

However, Riven’s deepening feelings for Lyriel also became his greatest vulnerability. While on a routine patrol, Riven couldn't resist the pull of his heart and diverted his force to visit Lyriel, longing for her presence and the solace she brought. He lingered longer than he should have, savoring every moment with her—the way her laughter filled the air, the warmth of her touch, and the way her golden hair caught the light as they strolled through the forest. But this tender moment of weakness led to a terrible oversight. Unbeknownst to Riven, the undead were advancing upon a nearby village. By the time he realized his grave mistake, it was too late. Riven led his force with a zealous fury, cutting through the undead ranks and driving them back with unmatched skill and determination. But despite his efforts, the village had already been overrun, its people slaughtered. The village lay in ruins, a testament to his failure to act when duty called. The blood of the innocent weighed heavily on his soul, and the realization that his love for Lyriel had caused him to falter and fail in his oath haunted him from that moment forward.

Riven stood before the elven council, his heart pounding with a mix of dread and guilt, as they pronounced their judgment. The court-martial was swift and unforgiving; he was publicly stripped of his rank, the once-proud insignia of a captain torn from his armor in front of his fellow soldiers. The sting of their disapproving glares was sharper than any blade, and the whispers of his failure echoed in his ears long after the proceedings ended. To be shamed in such a way, knowing he had failed his oath and let down not only his people but also the woman he loved, was a wound deeper than any he had ever suffered in battle. Lyriel, ever compassionate, did not see his actions in the same harsh light; she tried to comfort him, to remind him of the good he had done. But Riven couldn’t escape the suffocating weight of his own shame. The image of the destroyed village, the lives lost because of his moment of weakness, haunted him. The knowledge that he had failed his duty, that he had let his heart lead him astray, became a burden he couldn’t shake. Even Lyriel’s love couldn’t pierce through the cloud of self-loathing that settled over him, and the shame drove a wedge between them, as Riven withdrew further into himself, unable to face what he had become.

Riven sank deeper into despair and misery, finding no escape from the overwhelming guilt that consumed him. Each day felt like a slow, torturous descent, with no light at the end of the tunnel. Lyriel, ever gentle and loving, did all she could to comfort him, trying to pull him out of the darkness that had taken hold of his heart. But no matter her efforts, Riven remained inconsolable, his mind trapped in a cycle of self-blame and regret. He could see no future for himself in the elven kingdom, only the shadow of his failure looming large over every corner of his life. With no hope of redemption in sight and the weight of his shame growing heavier by the day, he made the heart-wrenching decision to leave Kyonin. Lyriel pleaded with him to stay, her voice thick with tears and desperation, but Riven’s resolve was unyielding. He promised her that he would return, but even as the words left his lips, they felt hollow, uncertain. Their parting was filled with an incalculable sadness, a deep and aching grief that came from not knowing if they would ever see each other again. Lyriel gave him a parting gift, an amulet, a symbol of her love. As Riven walked away, leaving behind the life and love he had once cherished, the future stretched out before him as a bleak and unforgiving road, with no clear destination in sight.

Riven journeyed northeast, leaving the familiar forests of Kyonin behind, his heart heavy with unresolved sorrow but driven by a desperate need to find redemption. However, his concept of redemption was still tied to external achievements—proving his worth through great deeds. The wild and untamed lands of the River Kingdoms called to him, a place where the rigid order of his homeland gave way to chaotic freedom and the chance to start anew. It was here in the Embeth Forest that he encountered a band of freedom fighters, "the Few", a group of hardened men and women dedicated to resisting the tyranny that plagued the region. Drawn to their cause and seeing in it a chance to atone for his past, Riven joined their ranks. His skills as a warrior, ranger, and his deep connection to nature quickly made him an invaluable member. He led daring raids against despotic lords, his arrows finding their marks with deadly precision, and his knowledge of the land turning the wilderness into an ally rather than an obstacle. Together, they struck fear into the hearts of petty tyrants, toppling oppressive regimes and bringing hope to the downtrodden. For a time, Riven found purpose in their cause, the thrill of the fight and the camaraderie of his fellow freedom fighters giving him a sense of belonging he had thought lost forever.

The turning point came when the leader of the freedom fighters was killed, and Rowan Half Dark, a ruthless and ambitious man, took his place. Under Rowan’s leadership, the group’s noble cause quickly deteriorated. What had once been a band of warriors fighting for justice became a band of common bandits, driven by greed and ruthlessness. Rowan led them down a dark path, targeting not just tyrants but anyone with wealth, blurring the line between freedom fighting and outright banditry. Before long, they were no longer champions of the oppressed but mercenaries for the very petty tyrants they once opposed, enforcing cruelty for coin. One tyrant that the Few aided was Irovetti of Pitax. Riven, trapped by his skills and loyalty, found himself sinking deeper into a life of moral compromise, far from the redemption he had once sought.

When "the Few" were ordered to start killing innocent men, women, and children, it was a line too far for Riven. The thought of slaughtering the defenseless was more than he could bear, and he refused outright, his conscience rebelling against the orders. This defiance marked him as a traitor in Rowan Half Dark’s eyes, and a hit was ordered on his life. In the dead of night, an assassin came for Riven, but his years of training and instincts saved him. After a fierce struggle, Riven defeated the would-be killer, and, knowing there was no turning back, he confronted Rowan. In a tense and bitter exchange, the two clashed, and Riven, driven by a mix of fury and desperation, killed Rowan on the spot. He fled into the wilds, pursued by the bandits he once called friends and King Irovetti’s men. But they could not catch him; Riven’s skills as a ranger and his deep knowledge of the land allowed him to disappear into the wilderness. Rumors circulated that Rowan’s sister Talia now led the Few and sought revenge and King Irovetti had not forgotten him. Now, alone and hunted, he wandered, carrying the weight of his past and the uncertain hope of finding a new path.

Riven wandered further east through Mivon, his heart heavy with the weight of his past, yet driven by a restless need to find meaning and redemption. It was during these solitary travels that he encountered Amara, a wandering priest of Sarenrae, the goddess of redemption and healing. Amara, seeing the pain in Riven's eyes, spoke to him of second chances, of the power to atone for one's sins and the hope of a new dawn after even the darkest night. These words touched something deep within Riven, rekindling a spark of hope he thought long extinguished. Yet, despite her wisdom, Riven remained focused on the idea that his redemption could only come from great deeds and victories—never recognizing that the true battle lay within himself. With renewed purpose, he continued his journey northeast through the vast untamed wilds of the Stolen Lands, where he found an unlikely companion in an orphaned young grizzly bear cub he named Brumble. The two formed a bond, traveling together through the dangerous, uncharted territories. Eventually, Riven's path led him to Restov, where he was summoned by the famed swordlord Jamandi Aldori. It was here that Riven saw an opportunity to fight for something greater, to perhaps, finally, redeem himself and fulfill his oath and his destiny.

Yet, deep down, Riven still hasn’t faced the hardest truth—that no quest will ever bring redemption. It is an internal process of forgiveness and self-acceptance. The true challenge for Riven lies in confronting his guilt and shame, and understanding that he must value himself and those he loves more than his quest for redemption. His relationships with himself, his friends, family and those he loves are his path to redemption. Until he makes this shift, his journey will remain unfinished, and the redemption he seeks will continue to elude him.
biography.catchphrases: "Redemption is given, not earned.", "Mercy for the broken, an arrow for the defiant.","We walk this road together", "I dare to hope"
biography.campaignNotes:
Since arriving in Brevoy at the summons of Jamandi Aldori in 4720, Riven quickly found himself thrust into a new and dangerous mission. Alongside a small band of adventurers, he was tasked with taming the Stolen Lands, a lawless region rife with banditry and dark magic. From the outset, Riven’s deep hatred of bandits drove him to confront these threats head-on. At Oleg’s Trading Post, he led a successful ambush against a group of bandits who had been extorting the local traders, sparing only a young man named Jake who showed signs of regret. This victory set the tone for Riven’s leadership, marked by both his relentless pursuit of justice and his capacity for mercy when warranted.

As Riven and his companions pressed deeper into the Stolen Lands, they encountered the infamous Stag Lord, a tyrant who ruled the region’s bandits with an iron fist. Riven, with his tactical mind and sharpshooting skills, played a crucial role in the plan to overthrow the Stag Lord. Scaling the walls of the Stag Lord’s fortress under cover of darkness, Riven took down bandits with well-aimed arrows and helped bring down the Stag Lord, symbolizing his ongoing fight against oppression. After this victory, the group founded the town of Nightmarch on the site of the Stag Lord’s keep, with Riven chosen as the leader due to his wisdom, pragmatism, and ability to unite people. This marked the beginning of a new chapter in his life, as he shifted from a wandering warrior to a ruler responsible for the safety and prosperity of his people.

Under Riven’s leadership, Nightmarch quickly grew into a thriving settlement, attracting settlers and establishing trade routes. However, the challenges were far from over. The town was plagued by undead rising from a nearby cemetery, leading Riven to battle these dark forces nightly with the help of his loyal companion, Brumble. Eventually, the party discovered that a cursed book held by a troll king was the source of the undead blight. Riven played a pivotal role in defeating the troll king and retrieving the book, bringing an end to the undead threat. His efforts expanded the fledgling kingdom, adding new towns like Leveton and Vesket, and solidifying his reputation as a protector and leader.

Despite these successes, Riven faced new and more insidious challenges. A powerful fey, Narissa, began influencing events in the region, leading to the rise of a dangerous cult and an attack by a supernaturally enormous owlbear. Riven’s deepening connection to primal magic and his growing responsibilities as a ruler were tested as he battled these threats. Through it all, he remained resolute, guiding the kingdom through trials of disease, disputes, and external threats, eventually uniting the towns into the kingdom of Freehaven. With a flag symbolizing liberty and strength, and a council of trusted advisors by his side, Riven has continued to lead with the same determination that drove him to Brevoy, but with a newfound sense of purpose rooted in the protection and prosperity of his people.
biography.allies:
Brumble
His loyal grizzly bear companion now retired from adventuring, Brumble is Riven’s steadfast friend especially when he needs it most

Pavel
The two share a bond over their similar love of nature and skills. Riven admires his courage and daring.

Astor
Riven relies on his wise counsel and his deep concern for the people. Riven admires his strength.

Ekundayo
A fellow ranger and ally, Riven respects him for his expertise and shared understanding of loss.

Linzi
Optimistic and dedicated to improving the kingdom’s image, Linzi’s loyalty and positivity ground Riven, but also make him miss Lyriel.

Akiros Ismort & Cassandra Thorne
Former bandits who sought redemption, becoming trusted allies, they share Riven's desire to atone for past mistakes and find a new purpose. They are commanders in Freehaven's army
biography.enemies:
King Irovetti of Pitax
Seeks Riven’s death for disrupting his expansionist schemes into Brevoy.

Talia and her Bandits
Seek Riven’s death for killing her brother, Rowan Half Dark, and dismantling their operations.

Enemies of Nightmarch and Rival Kingdoms
Harbor animosity toward Riven for his defense of Freehaven.

Possibly Narissa
May target Riven due to his growing connection with the fey and interference in her plans.

Undead, Demons, and Perversions of Nature
Are natural enemies of Riven, given his role in combating these threats.

description.value: When sounded, a signal whistle can be heard clearly up to half a mile away across open terrain.

This kit of bandages, herbs, and suturing tools is necessary for Medicine checks to @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.MHLuKy4nQO2Z4Am1]{Administer First Aid}, @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.TC7OcDa7JlWbqMaN]{Treat Disease}, @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.KjoCEEmPGTeFE4hh]{Treat Poison}, or @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.1kGNdIIhuglAjIp9]{Treat Wounds}. If you wear your healer's toolkit, you can draw and replace it as part of the action that uses it.

Crafted by Riven’s own hand from horn, yew, and sinew, this composite longbow has been his faithful companion from his first days as a ranger to his reign as king of Freehaven over the Stolen Lands. Its grip is worn smooth fitting its master's hand. Etched with runes of flame and astral power, it kindles with ember-light and starfire when drawn, loosing arrows with blinding speed.

With it, Riven proved himself the greatest archer of his age and slew King Irovetti, the monsterous owlbear, the bloom cult leader, Vordekai the lich, Armag Reborn, and many other terrors besides. More than a weapon, it carries something of Riven himself: his skill, his will, and his ferocity. Feywind stands as a living symbol of Freehaven’s strength.
These projectiles are the ammunition for bows. The shaft of an arrow is made of wood. It is stabilized in flight by fletching at one end and bears a metal head on the other.
A mix of flexible and molded boiled leather, a suit of this type of armor provides some protection with maximum flexibility.

You need a thieves' toolkit to @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.2EE4aF4SZpYf0R6H]{Pick Locks} or @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.cYdz2grcOcRt4jk6]{Disable Devices} (of some types) using the Thievery skill.

If your thieves' toolkit is broken, you can repair it by replacing the lock picks with @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.equipment-srd.Item.Sw7MBLASN3xK4Y44]{Replacement Picks} appropriate to your toolkit; this doesn't require using the Repair action. If you wear your thieves' toolkit, you can draw and replace it as part of the action that uses it.

As an ancient people, elves have seen great change and have the perspective that can come only from watching the arc of history. After leaving the world in ancient times, they returned to a changed land, and they still struggle to reclaim their ancestral homes, most notably from terrible demons that have invaded parts of their lands. To some, the elves are objects of awe—graceful and beautiful, with immense talent and knowledge. Among themselves, however, the elves place far more importance on personal freedom than on living up to these ideals.

@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.journals.JournalEntry.45SK8rdbbxvEHfMn.JournalEntryPage.3cfdiId1IoxLsF9V]{Elf}

In your long life, you've dabbled in many paths and many styles. A typical ancient elf is at least 100 years old, though you might be younger at the GM's discretion. Choose a class other than your own. You gain the multiclass dedication feat for that class, even though you don't meet its level prerequisite. You must still meet its other prerequisites to gain the feat.

Some rangers believe civilization wears down the soul, but still needs to be protected from wild creatures. Others say nature needs to be protected from the greedy, who wish to tame its beauty and plunder its treasures. You could champion either goal, or both. You might be a scout, tracker, or hunter of fugitives or beasts, haunting the edge of civilization or exploring the wilds. You know how to live off the land and are skilled at spotting and taking down both opportune prey and hated enemies.

@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.journals.JournalEntry.kzxu2dI7tFxv6Ix6.JournalEntryPage.gdEmPPRYhRai7I1N]{Ranger}

You have trained to become a skilled hunter and tracker, gaining an extra benefit when you Hunt Prey depending on the focus of your training. Choose a hunter's edge.

  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.classfeatures.Item.6v4Rj7wWfOH1882r]{Flurry}
  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.classfeatures.Item.NBHyoTrI8q62uDsU]{Outwit}
  • @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.classfeatures.Item.u6cBjqz2fiRBadBt]{Precision}
When you focus your attention on a single foe, you become unstoppable in your pursuit. You gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.JYi4MnsdFu618hPm]{Hunt Prey} action.
Your mental defenses grow stronger. Your proficiency rank for Will saves increases to expert.

You have trained to aim for your prey's weak points. The first time you hit your hunted prey in a round, you also deal 1d8 additional precision damage. (Precision damage increases the damage you already deal, using the same type, but is ineffective against creatures that lack vital organs or weak points.) At 11th level, the extra damage increases to 2d8 precision damage, and at 19th level, the extra damage increases to 3d8 precision damage.

You designate a single creature as your prey and focus your attacks against that creature. You must be able to see or hear the prey, or you must be tracking the prey during exploration.

You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to Perception checks when you @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.BlAOM2X92SI6HMtJ]{Seek} your prey and a +2 circumstance bonus to Survival checks when you @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.EA5vuSgJfiHH7plD]{Track} your prey. You also ignore the penalty for making ranged attacks within your second range increment against the prey you're hunting.

You can have only one creature designated as your prey at a time. If you use Hunt Prey against a creature when you already have a creature designated, the prior creature loses the designation and the new prey gains the designation. Your designation lasts until your next daily preparations.
You gain the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.journals.JournalEntry.vx5FGEG34AxI2dow.JournalEntryPage.DOc3Pf8wmVxanTIv#basic-spellcasting-feat]{Basic Spellcasting Feat} benefits.
You react more quickly than others can. You gain a +2 circumstance bonus to initiative rolls.

Requirements You are wielding a ranged weapon with reload 0.

You take two quick shots against the one you hunt. Make two Strikes against your prey with the required weapon. If both hit the same creature, combine their damage for the purpose of resistances and weaknesses. Apply your multiple attack penalty to each Strike normally.

You've trained with one of the ranger sects known as wardens, who practice a specialized type of primal magic. You gain your choice of one warden spell from these initial warden spells (or another you have access to).

@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.IWUe32Y5k2QFd7YQ]{Gravity Weapon}

@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.KIV2LqzS5KtqOItV]{Heal Companion}

@UUID[Compendium.pf2e.spells-srd.Item.u2uSeH6YSbK1ajTy]{Magic Hide}

Special You can take this feat multiple times, choosing a different initial warden spell each time.

You channel vital energy to heal the living or damage the undead. If the target is a willing living creature, you restore 1d8 Hit Points. If the target is undead, you deal that amount of vitality damage to it, and it gets a basic Fortitude save. The number of actions you spend when Casting this Spell determines its targets, range, area, and other parameters.

1 The spell has a range of touch.

2 (concentrate) The spell has a range of 30 feet. If you're healing a living creature, increase the Hit Points restored by 8.

3 (concentrate) You disperse vital energy in a @Template[type:emanation|distance:30]. This targets all living and undead creatures in the burst.

Heightened (+1) The amount of healing or damage increases by 1d8, and the extra healing for the 2-action version increases by 8.

You huddle behind cover or greater cover or deeper into concealment to become @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.iU0fEDdBp3rXpTMC]{Hidden}, rather than @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.1wQY3JYyhMYeeV2G]{Observed}. The GM rolls your [[/act hide]] check in secret and compares the result to the Perception DC of each creature you're observed by but that you have cover or greater cover against or are @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.DmAIPqOBomZ7H95W]{Concealed} from. You get a +2 circumstance bonus to your check if you have standard cover (or +4 from greater cover).

Success If the creature could see you, you're now @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.iU0fEDdBp3rXpTMC]{Hidden} from it instead of @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.1wQY3JYyhMYeeV2G]{Observed}. If you were hidden from or @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.VRSef5y1LmL2Hkjf]{Undetected} by the creature, you retain that condition.

If you successfully become hidden to a creature but then cease to have cover or greater cover against it or be concealed from it, you become observed again. You cease being hidden if you do anything except Hide, @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.VMozDqMMuK5kpoX4]{Sneak}, or @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.UHpkTuCtyaPqiCAB]{Step}. If you attempt to Strike a creature, the creature remains off-guard against that attack, and you then become observed. If you do anything else, you become observed just before you act unless the GM determines otherwise. The GM might allow you to perform a particularly unobtrusive action without being noticed, possibly requiring another Stealth check.

If a creature uses @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.BlAOM2X92SI6HMtJ]{Seek} to make you observed by it, you must successfully Hide to become hidden from it again.

You attempt to move to another place while becoming or staying undetected. Stride up to half your Speed. (You can use Sneak while Burrowing, Climbing, Flying, or Swimming instead of Striding if you have the corresponding movement type; you must move at half that Speed.)

At the end of your movement, the GM rolls your [[/act sneak]] check in secret and compares the result to the Perception DC of each creature you were @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.iU0fEDdBp3rXpTMC]{Hidden} from or @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.VRSef5y1LmL2Hkjf]{Undetected} by at the start of your movement. If you have cover or greater cover from the creature throughout your Stride, you gain the +2 circumstance bonus from cover (or +4 from greater cover) to your Stealth check. Because you're moving, the bonus increase from Taking Cover doesn't apply. You don't get to roll against a creature if, at the end of your movement, you neither are @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.DmAIPqOBomZ7H95W]{Concealed} from it nor have cover or greater cover against it. You automatically become observed by such a creature.

Success You're undetected by the creature during your movement and remain undetected by the creature at the end of it.

You become observed as soon as you do anything other than Hide, Sneak, or Step. If you attempt to Strike a creature, the creature remains @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.AJh5ex99aV6VTggg]{Off-Guard} against that attack, and you then become observed. If you do anything else, you become observed just before you act unless the GM determines otherwise. The GM might allow you to perform a particularly unobtrusive action without being noticed, possibly requiring another Stealth check. If you speak or make a deliberate loud noise, you become hidden instead of undetected.

If a creature uses @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.BlAOM2X92SI6HMtJ]{Seek} and you become hidden to it as a result, you must Sneak if you want to become undetected by that creature again.

Failure A telltale sound or other sign gives your position away, though you still remain unseen. You're hidden from the creature throughout your movement and remain so.

Critical Failure You're spotted! You're observed by the creature throughout your movement and remain so. If you're @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.conditionitems.Item.zJxUflt9np0q4yML]{Invisible} and were hidden from the creature, instead of being observed you're hidden throughout your movement and remain so.

You attempt a Stealth check to avoid notice while traveling at half speed. If you have the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.Imvu2RV2ggjJ2HZt]{Swift Sneak} feat, you can move at full Speed rather than half, but you still can't use another exploration activity while you do so. If you have the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.n0urrOL8YlnVBVRQ]{Legendary Sneak} feat, you can move at full Speed and use a second exploration activity. If you're Avoiding Notice at the start of an encounter, you usually roll a Stealth check instead of a Perception check both to determine your initiative and to see if the enemies notice you (based on their Perception DCs, as normal for Sneak, regardless of their initiative check results).

You follow tracks, moving at up to half your travel Speed, using the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.journals.JournalEntry.S55aqwWIzpQRFhcq.JournalEntryPage.YVLMgNxXTUQuDJgp]{Travel Speed} rules. After a successful check to Track, you can continue following the tracks at half your Speed without attempting additional checks for up to 1 hour.

In some cases, you might Track in an encounter. In this case, Track is a single action and doesn't have the exploration trait, but you might need to roll more often because you're in a tense situation. The GM determines how often you must attempt this check.

You attempt your Survival check when you start Tracking, once every hour you continue tracking, and any time something significant changes in the trail. The GM determines the DCs for such checks, depending on the freshness of the trail, the weather, and the type of ground.

Sample Track Tasks

  • Untrained the path of a large army following a road
  • Trained relatively fresh tracks of a rampaging bear through the plains
  • Expert a nimble panther's tracks through a jungle, tracks obscured by rainfall
  • Master tracks obscured by winter snow, tracks of a mouse or smaller creature, tracks left on surfaces that can't hold prints like bare rock
  • Legendary old tracks through a windy desert's sands, tracks obscured by a major blizzard or hurricane

Success You find the trail or continue to follow the one you're already following.

Failure You lose the trail but can try again after a 1-hour delay.

Critical Failure You lose the trail and can't try again for 24 hours.

You @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.actionspf2e.Item.BlAOM2X92SI6HMtJ]{Seek} meticulously for hidden doors, concealed hazards, and so on. You can usually make an educated guess as to which locations are best to check and move at half speed, but if you want to be thorough and guarantee you checked everything, you need to travel at a Speed of no more than 300 feet per minute, or 150 feet per minute to ensure you check everything before you walk into it. You can always move more slowly while Searching to cover the area more thoroughly, and the @UUID[Compendium.pf2e.feats-srd.Item.GdZLxDtFXaQI3Fop]{Expeditious Search} feat increases these maximum Speeds. If you come across a secret door, item, or hazard while Searching, the GM will attempt a free secret check to Seek to see if you notice the hidden object or hazard. In locations with many objects to search, you have to stop and spend significantly longer to search thoroughly.

You can use a skill to earn money during downtime. You must be trained in the skill to do so. This takes time to set up, and your income depends on your proficiency rank and how lucrative a task you can find. Because this process requires a significant amount of time and involves tracking things outside the progress of adventures, it won't come up in every campaign. The most typical ways to earn income, detailed further in this section are:

  • Crafting goods for the market (Crafting)
  • Practicing a Trade (Lore)
  • Staging a Performance (Performance)

In some cases, the GM might let you use a different skill to Earn Income through specialized work. Usually, this is scholarly work, such as using Religion in a monastery to study old texts—but giving sermons at a church would still fall under Performance instead of Religion. You also might be able to use physical skills to make money, such as using Acrobatics to perform feats in a circus or Thievery to pick pockets. If you're using a skill other than Crafting, Lore, or Performance, the DC tends to be significantly higher.

Earn Income

Downtime

You use one of your skills to make money during downtime. The GM assigns a task level representing the most lucrative job available. You can search for lower-level tasks, with the GM determining whether you find any. Sometimes you can attempt to find better work than the initial offerings, though this takes time and requires using the Diplomacy skill to Gather Information, doing some research, or socializing.

When you take on a job, the GM secretly sets the DC of your skill check. After your first day of work, you roll to determine your earnings. You gain an amount of income based on your result, the task's level, and your proficiency rank (as listed on the Income Earned table).

You can continue working at the task on subsequent days without needing to roll again. For each day you spend after the first, you earn the same amount as the first day, up until the task's completion. The GM determines how long you can work at the task. Most tasks last a week or two, though some can take months or even years.

Sample Earn Income Tasks

These examples use Alcohol Lore to work in a bar or Legal Lore to perform legal work.

  • Trained bartend, do legal research
  • Expert curate drink selection, present minor court cases
  • Master run a large brewery, present important court cases
  • Legendary run an international brewing franchise, present a case in Hell's courts

Crafting Goods for the Market [Crafting]

Using Crafting, you can work at producing common items for the market. It's usually easy to find work making basic items whose level is 1 or 2 below your settlement's level.

Higher-level tasks represent special commissions, which might require you to Craft a specific item using the Craft downtime activity and sell it to a buyer at full Price. These opportunities don't occur as often and might have special requirements—or serious consequences if you disappoint a prominent client.

Practicing a Trade [Lore]

You apply the practical benefits of one of your Lore specialties during downtime by practicing your trade. This is most effective for Lore specialties such as business, law, or sailing, where there's high demand for workers. The GM might increase the DC or determine only low-level tasks are available if you're attempting to use an obscure Lore skill to earn income. You might also need specialized tools to accept a job, like mining tools to work in a mine or a merchant's scale to buy and sell valuables in a market.

Staging a Performance [Performance]

You perform for an audience to make money. The available audiences determine the level of your task, since more discerning audiences are harder to impress but provide a bigger payout. The GM determines the task level based on the audiences available. Performing for a typical audience of commoners on the street is a level 0 task, but a performance for a group of artisans with more refined tastes might be a 2nd- or 3rd-level task, and ones for merchants, nobility, and royalty are increasingly higher level.

Your degree of success determines whether you moved your audience and whether you were rewarded with applause or rotten fruit.

Ending or Interrupting Tasks

When a task you're doing is complete, or if you stop in the middle of one, you normally have to find a new task if you want to keep Earning Income. For instance, if you quit your job working at the docks, you'll need to find another place of employment instead of picking up where you left off. This usually takes 1 day or more of downtime looking for leads on new jobs.

However, you might pause a task due to an adventure or event that wouldn't prevent you from returning to the old job later. The GM might decide that you can pick up where you left off, assuming the task hasn't been completed by others in your absence. Whether you roll a new skill check when you resume is also up to the GM. Generally speaking, if you had a good initial roll and want to keep it, you can, but if you had a bad initial roll, you can't try for a better one by pausing to do something else. If your statistics changed during the break—usually because you leveled up while adventuring—you can attempt a new check.

Retraining offers a way to alter some of your character choices, which is helpful when you want to take your character in a new direction or change decisions that didn't meet your expectations. You can retrain feats, skills, and some selectable class features. You can't retrain your ancestry, heritage, background, class, or ability scores. You can't perform other downtime activities while retraining.

Retraining usually requires you to spend time learning from a teacher, whether that entails physical training, studying at a library, or falling into shared magical trances. Your GM determines whether you can get proper training or whether something can be retrained at all. In some cases, you'll have to pay your instructor.

Some abilities can be difficult or impossible to retrain (for instance, a sorcerer can retrain their bloodline only in extraordinary circumstances).

When retraining, you generally can't make choices you couldn't make when you selected the original option. For instance, you can't exchange a 2nd-level skill feat for a 4th-level one, or for one that requires prerequisites you didn't meet at the time you took the original feat. If you don't remember whether you met the prerequisites at the time, ask your GM to make the call. If you cease to meet the prerequisites for an ability due to retraining, you can't use that ability. You might need to retrain several abilities in sequence in order to get all the abilities you want.

Feats

You can spend a week of downtime retraining to swap out one of your feats. Remove the old feat and replace it with another of the same type. For example, you could swap a skill feat for another skill feat, but not for a wizard feat.

Skills

You can spend a week of downtime retraining to swap out one of your skill increases. Reduce your proficiency rank in the skill losing its increase by one step and increase your proficiency rank in another skill by one step. The new proficiency rank has to be equal to or lower than the proficiency rank you traded away. For instance, if your bard is a master in Performance and Stealth, and an expert in Occultism, you could reduce the character's proficiency in Stealth to expert and become a master in Occultism, but you couldn't reassign that skill increase to become legendary in Performance. Keep track of your level when you reassign skill increases; the level at which your skill proficiencies changed can influence your ability to retrain feats with skill prerequisites.

You can also spend a week to retrain an initial trained skill you gained during character creation.

Class Features

You can change a class feature that required a choice, making a different choice instead. This lets you change a druid order or a wizard school, for example. The GM will tell you how long this takes-always at least a month.
